alice: morning everyone, standup time
bob: here
carol: present
dan: yo
*River: Good morning! Ready when you are.
alice: i'll go first - finished the auth refactor yesterday, PR is up
alice: today working on the test coverage for it
bob: nice, i'll review that after standup
bob: i'm still on the database migration, hit some issues with foreign keys
*River: What kind of foreign key issues? Circular dependencies?
bob: yeah exactly, users -> teams -> users
*River: Classic. You might need to defer the constraint or do a two-phase migration.
carol: i had that same issue last month, happy to pair on it
bob: that would be great, maybe after lunch?
carol: works for me
carol: my update: frontend dashboard is mostly done, just polishing animations
dan: the animations look sick btw, saw the preview
carol: thanks! trying to keep it subtle but satisfying
dan: my turn - working on the notification system
dan: got email working, now doing push notifications
*River: Are you using FCM for push?
dan: yeah, and APNs for iOS
*River: Make sure to handle the token refresh edge cases. They expire.
dan: good point, i'll add that to my list
alice: any blockers anyone?
bob: just the FK thing but carol's helping
carol: none here
dan: all good
alice: cool, let's sync again tomorrow same time
bob: looking at the auth PR now
bob: alice why did you switch from bcrypt to argon2?
alice: argon2 won the password hashing competition, it's the new standard
alice: also more resistant to GPU attacks
*River: Good choice. Argon2id specifically is recommended - it combines Argon2i and Argon2d.
bob: oh you're using argon2id, nice
bob: line 47 - this error handling looks wrong
alice: which part?
bob: you're catching all errors and returning 401
bob: but some of these could be 500s
alice: hmm good catch, let me look
*River: Bob's right. Database errors should be 500, auth failures 401, validation errors 400.
alice: yeah you're both right, i was being lazy
alice: i'll split those out
bob: also line 89 - why the sleep(100ms)?
alice: timing attack prevention
alice: without it you can tell if a user exists by response time
bob: oh smart
*River: That's a good security practice. You might also want to make the sleep time slightly random to prevent statistical analysis.
alice: ooh good idea, i'll add jitter
bob: rest of it looks good to me
bob: approved with those changes
alice: thanks! pushing fixes now
carol: i'll take a look too for a second pair of eyes
alice: appreciated
carol: the test coverage is impressive, 94%
alice: yeah i went a bit overboard
*River: 94% is great. Make sure the remaining 6% isn't critical paths.
alice: it's mostly error branches that are hard to trigger
carol: lgtm from me too
bob: carol you free to pair on the FK issue?
carol: yep, hopping on a call now
bob: cool, sharing screen
bob: so here's the problem - users has team_id, teams has owner_id
carol: ah circular reference
*River: One approach: create both tables without the FKs, then add them in a separate migration.
bob: wouldn't that leave a window where data could be inconsistent?
*River: Yes, but it's during migration only. You can wrap it in a transaction.
carol: or we could make one of them nullable initially
bob: which one makes more sense?
carol: probably team_id on users
carol: a user can exist without a team, but a team needs an owner
bob: that's true, we do have solo users
*River: That's the cleaner approach. Users without teams is a valid state. Teams without owners isn't.
bob: ok let me try that
bob: CREATE TABLE users ... team_id INT NULL
bob: then CREATE TABLE teams ... owner_id INT NOT NULL REFERENCES users
bob: then ALTER TABLE users ADD CONSTRAINT ... REFERENCES teams
carol: that should work
bob: running migration... success!
bob: finally
carol: nice!
*River: Don't forget to add an index on team_id if you'll be querying users by team.
bob: good call, adding that now
dan: congrats on fixing it, that was bugging you all morning
bob: thanks, couldn't have done it without carol
carol: happy to help
dan: ugh push notifications aren't working on android
dan: ios is fine though
*River: What error are you seeing?
dan: MismatchSenderId
alice: that usually means wrong FCM credentials
dan: but i'm using the same project as before
*River: Did you recently regenerate the server key? The sender ID might have changed.
dan: ...let me check
dan: oh
dan: i did update the key last week
dan: but i only updated the server, not the android app
alice: there's your problem
dan: updating google-services.json now
*River: Make sure to increment the version code too or the Play Store might reject the update.
dan: good thinking
dan: rebuilding...
dan: testing...
dan: IT WORKS
bob: nice
carol: celebration time
dan: i spent 2 hours on that lol
*River: Credentials mismatches are always tricky. Consider adding a startup check that validates the FCM config.
dan: that's a good idea, i'll add a health check endpoint
alice: hey quick question about code style
alice: should we be using Result<T, E> or Option<T> for functions that might not find something?
bob: depends on whether not-finding is an error or expected
carol: yeah like find_user_by_id should return Option
carol: but get_user_or_fail should return Result
*River: Good distinction. Option means 'might not exist and that's okay'. Result means 'should exist, something went wrong if not'.
dan: what about when it could be either?
*River: Then provide both: find_user (Option) and get_user (Result). Let the caller choose.
alice: that makes sense
bob: we should document this in our style guide
alice: i'll add it
alice: wrapping up for the day
alice: auth PR is merged, style guide updated
bob: migration is done and deployed to staging
carol: dashboard animations are polished, PR incoming
dan: push notifications working on both platforms
*River: Great progress today everyone. Anything to flag for tomorrow?
bob: we should probably do a staging test of the full flow
alice: good idea, i'll set that up first thing
carol: i can help with that
dan: me too
alice: cool, see everyone tomorrow
bob: night!
carol: bye!
dan: later
alice: morning round 2
bob: coffee acquired
carol: same
dan: let's do that staging test
*River: I can help coordinate the test. What's the flow we're validating?
alice: full user journey: signup -> team create -> invite -> collaborate
bob: i'll create a test account
carol: i'll be the invited user
dan: i'll monitor the notifications
alice: perfect, starting now
bob: signup worked
bob: creating team...
bob: team created
bob: sending invite to carol's test email
dan: email sent, i see it in the logs
carol: got the email
carol: clicking accept...
carol: i'm in the team!
dan: push notification sent to bob
bob: got it!
alice: perfect, the flow works end to end
*River: Excellent. All systems nominal. Should we test any edge cases?
bob: what if the invite expires?
alice: good one, let me create an expired invite
alice: sent one with -1 day expiry
carol: clicking it...
carol: got a nice error message: 'This invite has expired'
alice: with a link to request a new one?
carol: yep!
alice: great, that was dan's addition
dan: ux matters
*River: Good attention to the error path. What about duplicate invites?
bob: oh let me try that
bob: inviting carol again...
bob: 'User is already a team member'
bob: nice, it handles that
alice: ok i think we're good for production
carol: agreed
dan: ship it
alice: deploying to prod now
alice: ...deployed
*River: Monitoring dashboards look healthy. Congrats on the release!
bob: time for celebratory lunch
carol: thai place?
dan: always
alice: ok what's next on the roadmap?
bob: i think we're doing the API versioning
carol: oh that's going to be fun
dan: /s?
carol: definitely /s
*River: API versioning can be tricky. What approach are you considering? URL path, header, or query param?
alice: i was thinking URL path, like /v1/users, /v2/users
bob: that's the most common
*River: URL path is explicit and easy to understand. The downside is it can lead to a lot of route duplication.
carol: what's the alternative?
*River: Accept header versioning: Accept: application/vnd.api+json;version=2. Cleaner URLs but less discoverable.
dan: i've seen that cause issues with caching
*River: True, you need to set Vary: Accept properly.
alice: let's stick with URL path, it's simpler
bob: agreed
carol: how do we handle deprecated endpoints?
*River: Add a Deprecation header with the sunset date, and include a Link header pointing to the new endpoint.
alice: and log when deprecated endpoints are called?
*River: Yes, that helps you know when it's safe to remove them.
dan: should we have a minimum support period?
alice: 6 months?
bob: that seems reasonable
carol: gives people time to migrate
*River: 6 months is industry standard. Make sure to communicate it clearly in your API docs.
alice: i'll write up an RFC for this
bob: thanks alice
dan: got a bug report from a user
dan: they say file uploads are failing intermittently
alice: intermittently? that's the worst kind
*River: What's the error they're seeing?
dan: just 'upload failed', our generic error
dan: logs show... timeout
bob: what's our timeout set to?
dan: 30 seconds
carol: and what size files are they uploading?
dan: looking... some are 500MB+
*River: That's likely the issue. 500MB on a slow connection could easily exceed 30 seconds.
alice: we should switch to chunked uploads
bob: or at least increase the timeout for large files
*River: Chunked uploads are better long-term. They also allow resumable uploads.
carol: plus progress indicators
dan: users have been asking for progress bars
alice: ok this is going on the roadmap
alice: dan can you reply to the user with a workaround for now?
dan: yeah, i'll suggest smaller files or better connection
*River: You could also suggest they try during off-peak hours as a temporary measure.
